patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH] mm: bypass swap readahead for zswap

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH] mm: bypass swap readahead for zswap

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch bypasses swap readahead for zswap, an in-memory compression backend similar to zram. The current implementation of swap readahead is optimized for traditional disk-based swap devices and can cause issues like inflated anon residency, page cache thrashing, and increased fault latency when applied to an in-memory backend. By skipping readahead for zswap, the patch aims to improve performance by reducing unnecessary prefetching of pages that are not needed. The results from testing this bypass show significant improvements in build time, swap-in/out rates, and reduced swap readahead hits.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Nhat Pham expressed enthusiasm for exploring zswap optimizations and encouraged further investigation into potential improvements.

Reviewer Nhat Pham suggested modifying the readahead logic to skip zswap and sync-io devices at each slot in the readahead window, allowing IO work to be submitted while sparing synchronous decompression work that might affect latency.

Reviewer Nhat Pham questioned the performance benefits of bypassing swap readahead for zswap, pointing out that if the pages are needed, it transfers work from future page faults to the current one, and if they're not needed, it wastes cycles.
review_comments.raw_body:
You're playing with zswap? Nice :)

It does feel like we're leaving some obvious wins on the table with
zswap here, so any ideas are much appreciated :)

---

Yeah I can see the edge case you and Yosry brought up here. As we move
towards supporting multiple swap backends in the same system, that can
certainly happen. I'm hoping that locality will bail us out - pages
close together in virtual address space hopefully have similar
lifetime, access temperature, compressibility, and will go to the same
swap backend, etc. - but who knows :)

What if we still follow the readahead logic, but at each slot in the
readahead window, we check if the entry is owned by zswap or is of a
sync-io devices, in which case we skip the readahead...?

That way, we will still submit the IO work, but spare the ones that
require synchronous decompression work, which might affect latency...?

---

Hmm so what your proposal buys us is, if we're already doing readahead
(synchronously), might as well install the pages in the window into
the page tables? :)

Could you explain to me how does this improve performance? If the
pages are needed, we are transferring some work from future page
faults to the current page fault. If the pages are not needed, then we
are just wasting cycles right?

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 5/5] btrfs: use GFP_NOWAIT when inhibiting eb writeback

version_history.subject: [PATCH 5/5] btrfs: use GFP_NOWAIT when inhibiting eb writeback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_inhibit_eb_writeback function to use GFP_NOWAIT instead of GFP_NOFS when allocating memory for inhibiting writeback on an extent buffer. This change is made because the allocation is already best effort and occurs while holding locks, making NOWAIT a suitable choice.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 4/5] btrfs: probe with GFP_NOWAIT for tree block readahead

version_history.subject: [PATCH 4/5] btrfs: probe with GFP_NOWAIT for tree block readahead

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs readahead mechanism to use GFP_NOWAIT when reading tree blocks, allowing it to fail instead of blocking in direct reclaim if allocation fails. This change is intended to prevent painful stalls and lock contention caused by readahead getting stuck in direct reclaim.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 3/5] btrfs: enable unlocked NOFAIL retry for eb allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/5] btrfs: enable unlocked NOFAIL retry for eb allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch enables unlocked NOFAIL retry for eb allocations in the Btrfs file system. It introduces a new preallocation struct to carry allocation and signal requirements, allowing callers to attempt NOWAIT allocations if supported by the allocator. If the initial allocation fails, it signals that preallocation is required, enabling the caller to perform the necessary allocation and retry with the preallocated resources. The patch also notes that two GFP_NOFS allocations remain under lock and cannot be preallocated due to limitations in the filemap xarray.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/5] btrfs: add struct btrfs_eb_prealloc
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new structure called `struct btrfs_eb_prealloc` to carry the extent_buffer and btrfs_folio_state needed for NOFAIL allocations with retries outside the critical section. The existing allocation pathways are refactored to use this new struct, but no functional changes are made. Empty prealloc structs are wired in from callers. This is part of a larger series of patches to support NOFAIL allocations.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/5] btrfs: factor init_extent_buffer from __alloc_extent_buffer
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to refactor the initialization of extent buffers in Btrfs, allowing for preallocation and simplifying the code paths. The first patch factors out the initialization from the allocation function, setting the stage for future changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm/memory: add memory_block_aligned_range() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new helper function called `memory_block_aligned_range()` to the Linux kernel's memory management code. The function takes an input range and aligns its start address up and end address down to memory block boundaries, which is required for memory hotplug operations. This helper is added as a common function in `<linux/memory.h>` and is used by the `dax/kmem` driver to ensure that memory ranges are properly aligned. The patch also updates the `dax/kmem` code to use this new helper function instead of manually aligning the range. The goal of this patch is to simplify the process of handling memory hotplug operations and reduce the risk of errors due to misaligned ranges.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm/memory_hotplug: pass online_type to online_memory_block() via arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `online_memory_block()` function to accept an online type via its argument, rather than calling a default function internally. This change prepares for callers to specify explicit online types.

review_comments.author: Gupta, Pankaj
review_comments.summary: Approved the patch with a Reviewed-by tag, indicating that it is technically sound and meets kernel coding standards.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm/memory_hotplug: export mhp_get_default_online_type
series_items.patch_summary: This patch exports the `mhp_get_default_online_type` function from the Linux kernel's memory hotplug module, allowing drivers to use it even when hotplug is disabled. The function returns a default online type of `MMOP_OFFLINE`, which is non-destructive and suitable for DAX use cases. This change enables drivers like CXL to build and use DAX without requiring hotplug as a predicate. The patch also adds an inline implementation for the function in case it's not enabled by CONFIG_MEMORY_HOTPLUG.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm/memory_hotplug: add __add_memory_driver_managed() with online_type arg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new interface to the Linux kernel's memory hotplug functionality, allowing drivers to select their preferred online type (ZONE_NORMAL or ZONE_MOVABLE) when adding memory. The CXL driver is the primary motivator for this change, as it needs to plumb a preferred policy through the dax kmem driver. The patch refactors existing APIs and adds new helpers to support this feature, while ensuring that only known in-tree modules can override the system-wide onlining policy.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm/memory_hotplug: offline_and_remove_memory_ranges()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function called offline_and_remove_memory_ranges() that allows callers to manage multiple contiguous memory ranges as one operation under a single lock. The function processes the ranges in two phases: first, it offlines every block of every range; second, it removes the ranges only if all ranges are offline. If any offline fails, the whole operation is reverted, providing atomicity and resolving potential race conditions between system daemons trying to manage the same resource.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] dax: plumb hotplug online_type through dax
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows drivers that use the dax_kmem driver to specify an auto-online policy when creating a DAX device. It adds a new field, 'online_type', to the DAX device creation path, which can be used to override the system default online policy. The system default is still used if no explicit policy is specified, and this change does not introduce any behavioral changes for existing callers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] dax/kmem: extract hotplug/hotremove helper functions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the kmem probe and remove functions by extracting helper functions to manage memory hotplug operations. The new helpers, dax_kmem_init_resources, dax_kmem_cleanup_resources, dax_kmem_do_hotplug, and dax_kmem_do_hotremove, enable future extensions for more granular control over memory hotplug. This refactoring is necessary to support hot-add functionality by separating resource management from hotunplug/cleanup operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] dax/kmem: add sysfs interface for atomic whole-device hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysfs interface to enable atomic whole-device hotplug operations for DAX kmem devices. It introduces a new 'state' attribute that allows the device to be transitioned between different states, including unplugged, online, and offline. The patch also includes changes to handle legacy auto_online_blocks=offline policy and prevents deadlock during unbind operation. The atomic whole-device hotplug operation is achieved through a single call to dax_kmem_do_hotremove(), which collects all added ranges and offlines/removes them in one operation.

review_comments.summary: Reviewer Hannes Reinecke questioned the need to treat 'unbind' as a given thing and suggested disallowing unbind when memory is online, recommending that admins offline the memory first using the new sysfs knob.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author acknowledges that unbind is a violent operation and cannot fail, but still wants to prevent deadlocks by leaking resources if the device is online at unbind time. The author plans to reinstate legacy behavior when the state is not UNPLUGGED.
review_comments.raw_body:
No.  Unbind is a violent operation - unbinds cannot fail, and a
straight, uncoordinated unbind is essentially a `--force` flag:
the admin accepts the risks.

To your point, the admin either does the nice thing are they
muck up the system.

But we should still try to do something sane to defend the kernel,
in this case we should try to prevent that task from becoming
deadlocked.  The only way to do that is to leak the resources.

I'm making a small modification to this code to reinstate the
legacy behavior when "state!=UNPLUGGED".

~Gregory

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] selftests/dax: add dax/kmem hotplug sysfs regression test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a selftest to the Linux kernel's dax subsystem, specifically targeting the kmem hotplug sysfs interface. The test checks that the 'state' attribute of a kmem-backed dax device transitions correctly between unplugged, online, and online_movable states. It also verifies that force-unbinding does not deadlock and that invalid input is rejected. Additionally, it tests a specific regression scenario where re-onlineing after unplug must re-reserve per-range resources to ensure memory removal.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds atomic whole-device hotplug control to the dax kmem driver via a sysfs 'state' attribute, allowing for transitions across entire regions of memory to be performed atomically and safely. The current implementation has a race condition between offlining and removing memory blocks, which this patch aims to solve by introducing a new ABI with state names mirroring per-block memoryX/state attributes. The series also includes mm preparation changes to support the new hotplug control, including adding helpers for online type management and atomic offline+remove of multiple ranges. The dax/kmem feature is updated to plumb online_type through its device creation path and extract hotplug/hotremove into helper functions. A selftest is added to regression test the new attribute.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes the SVC_POOL_* enum and related code, hardcoding the pool mode to pernode (one pool per NUMA node) as it is considered the best choice today. The percpu mode, which created excessive pools, is removed along with its auto-selection logic. The module parameter and netlink interfaces are preserved for backward compatibility. If pool map allocation fails, service creation now fails instead of silently falling back to a single global pool.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that a server could end up with pools having no threads due to the way svc_set_num_threads() distributes threads across pools, and asked if there's a way for an admin to avoid empty pools on a server running fewer threads than NUMA nodes. He also pointed out that the documentation still presents the four pool modes as distinct, including global mode which is now removed.
review_comments.raw_body:
With one pool per node now mandatory, can a server end up with pools
that have no threads?  svc_set_num_threads() spreads nrservs evenly
across the pools:

net/sunrpc/svc.c:svc_set_num_threads() {
	unsigned int base = nrservs / serv->sv_nrpools;
	unsigned int remain = nrservs % serv->sv_nrpools;
	...
}

so when nrservs is smaller than the number of NUMA nodes with CPUs,
base is 0 and the trailing pools get zero threads.

Work is still routed to the per-node pool selected from the running
CPU:

net/sunrpc/svc_xprt.c:svc_xprt_enqueue() {
	pool = svc_pool_for_cpu(xprt->xpt_server);
	...
	lwq_enqueue(&xprt->xpt_ready, &pool->sp_xprts);
	svc_pool_wake_idle_thread(pool);
}

and svc_pool_wake_idle_thread() only consults that one pool's idle
list, with no fallback to a sibling pool.  If a connection lands on a
node whose pool has no threads, does its transport sit on sp_xprts and
never get serviced?

The old global default kept a single pool, so this configuration could
not arise without an explicit pernode/percpu selection.  With global
mode removed, is there a way left for an admin to avoid empty pools on
a server that runs fewer threads than it has NUMA nodes, or should the
thread distribution or the enqueue path guarantee work only reaches a
populated pool?

Separately, the pool_mode knob is preserved but every accepted value
now behaves as pernode.  Documentation/admin-guide/kernel-parameters.txt
still presents the four modes as distinct:

	auto	    the server chooses an appropriate mode
		    automatically using heuristics
	global	    a single global pool contains all CPUs
	percpu	    one pool for each CPU
	pernode	    one pool for each NUMA node (equivalent
		    to global on non-NUMA machines)

and states the option "will affect which CPUs will do NFS serving."
The patch should update this documentation.


-- 
Chuck Lever

The reviewer suggested initializing the count field of the svc_pool_map structure to 0 explicitly to make it clear there are no references, and also recommended renaming the count field to refcount for clarity.

Reviewer NeilBrown agreed that hardcoding pool_mode to pernode and removing other modes is a worthwhile change, but suggested that the minimum number of threads should be one per node instead of one total.

"!strncmp" is one most disliked constructs....

---

so it is obvious what is being counted, but that doesn't need to be part

---

Regarding Chucks comments, I think a minimum of one thread per node,
rather than a minimum of one thread total, is the sensible approach.
Of course if there exist memory-only nodes, they wouldn't need a thread.

Thanks,
NeilBrown

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v1 1/3] iomap: add ->iomap_next() and iomap_process() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new callback function called ->iomap_next() to the iomap iteration mechanism, which replaces the existing ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() callbacks. The new callback allows performance-critical callers to inline the iteration with a fixed callback, improving efficiency. A helper function called iomap_process() is also added to simplify the logic needed in an ->iomap_next() implementation. This change enables filesystems to be converted one at a time from using the legacy ->iomap_begin()/->iomap_end() path to the new ->iomap_next() callback.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v1 2/3] xfs: convert read and buffered write iomap ops to ->iomap_next()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the read and buffered write iomap operations for XFS to use the new ->iomap_next() callback, which simplifies the code by removing redundant functions and leveraging the iomap_process helper function. The change does not introduce any functional changes but rather refactors the existing implementation. This is a step towards migrating all XFS iomap operations to use the ->iomap_next() callback.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v1 3/3] xfs: example of devirtualizing buffered write iomap callbacks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch is an example of devirtualizing buffered write iomap callbacks in the XFS file system. It removes indirect calls by inlining callback functions and allows the compiler to make direct calls, reducing overhead. The patch modifies several files, including iomap/buffered-io.c, iomap/iter.c, xfs/xfs_file.c, and others, adding new functions and modifying existing ones to use the devirtualized callbacks. The goal is to improve performance by eliminating indirect function calls.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series converts the iomap code to use an in-iter model, where a single callback function is used for both beginning and ending I/O operations. The conversion involves merging two existing functions into one and adding a helper function to process I/O operations. An example of devirtualizing the callbacks on a hot path is also provided to avoid indirect calls through struct iomap_ops. The patch series raises questions about whether this conversion is worth it, given that indirect calls are cheap on modern hardware.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)

review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks Gao. I don't think the callback and helper models are as far
apart as it might look though after the ->iomap_next() change. Once
all the callers have been converted, there'll be no iomap_ops

callers own the iter and drive the loop to get rid of indirect calls -

out into helper functions, which each caller would then have to put
together / reassemble. It seems like a lot of duplicate code and I'm
not sure I see the benefit of open-coding that when none of the
callers right now need special logic interjected between the
fine-grained helpers and and their callback. imo it seems more

review_comments.author: Gao Xiang
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Gao Xiang noted that the patch introduces a potential deadlock scenario where iomap_next() is called while holding the folio lock, and requested that the lock be dropped before calling try_to_unmap().

reviewer noted that the patch does not fully align with their original in-iter direction and provided a specific example of the design model difference they are concerned about

Reviewer Gao Xiang noted that the recent iomap implementation is still callback-based and requires arranging mapping logic into unique callbacks, which can be inflexible for filesystems needing additional information. He suggested a high-level fs helper library to wrap up page cache management, IO management, and other operations, allowing for more flexibility in handling different types of I/O.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

On 2026/6/26 05:33, Joanne Koong wrote:

---

Thanks for the reply.

I partially agree since it's much closer, but what I meant is
the design model difference as below.

---

If you take a look at the initial commit of iomap ae259a9c8593
("fs: introduce iomap infrastructure"), the starting point was
to provide `struct iomap_ops` (two callbacks) to get the filesystem
extent mapping and unique entrypoints: iomap_file_buffered_write(),
iomap_page_mkwrite() for buffer writes (write and mmap write).

And then iomap applies to DAX, direct I/O, writeback, buffer read,
etc.  basically for block-based filesystems, and that is why
`struct iomap_ops` is always _one important part of iomap core_
until FUSE adapted.


But if we take a look at the overall requirement, I think what we
need is to provide an easy-use high-level fs helper library to
wrap up the following stuffs:

  1. page cache management (mainly sub-page tracking) and DAX pages;

  2. IO management (buffer I/O, direct I/O, DAX access);

  3. others (like fiemap, bmap, lseek, etc.)


For example, most block filesystems could use them of all, and for
FUSE and networking filesystem, basically they only need 1 and
maybe partial 3.

The recent implementation (including ->iomap_next()), is still
a callback-based model, the main loop is inside `fs/iomap` itself,
and you have to:

  - still arrange the mapping logic into an unique callback, which
is .iomap_next() now, if the mapping logic needs more information
rather than `struct inode *`, they have to add private pointer
somewhere.

  - Take a recent example (buffered read refactoring), need
    `struct iomap_read_folio_ctx` for FUSE and maybe other future
    networking filesystems to callback into their filesystem to
    provide new I/O contexts and submit I/Os.

But actually take a simple example of iomap_read_folio, you:
could change into (just a very rough sketch):

  xxxfs_read_folio {
    struct xxxxfs_private priv;

    iomap_iter(&iter) {   /// it could be a macro
        if (iomap_iter_outofrange(&iter))
		xxxfs_map_extent(&iter, &priv);

        // to maintain the page cache <-> struct iomap_folio_state
        // and handle some type of mapping
        if (iomap_read_folio_iter(&iter) == NEED_NEW_IO_CONTEXT) {
            xxxfs_new_io (&iter, &priv);
        }
    }
    if (xxxfs_if_any_available_io(&iter, &priv))
	xxxfs_submit_io(&iter, &priv);
}

In this model, I really think `struct iomap_read_folio_ctx` and
similar doesn't need any more (another set of callback avoidness),
and both xxxfs_map_extent() and xxxfs_new_io()/ xxxfs_submit_io()
can pass in their per-fs private without need to bother
the iomap-core to add various `private` pointers.

and in the iomap_iter() loop you can omit/change/add any part if
it doesn't fulfill to the filesystem timing.

As for "a lot of duplicate code", I think with proper new designs,
the common part could be wrapped up into macros (for example,
for bdev filesystems, xxxfs_new_io() and xxxfs_submit_io() calls
can be wrapped up into macros, and only leave:

	if (iomap_iter_outofrange(&iter))
		xxxfs_map_extent(&iter, &priv);"

can be customized for each fs as the common macro helpers.

It's just my rough thought, as I said in the previous year, I
still don't have a strong tendency but it's my suggestion over
these years.

Anyway since you're actively working/changing on iomap, so
it's fine to just respect your ideas/efforts if you don't
think it's worth, but as a record of my thoughts here again.

Thanks,
Gao Xiang

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig expressed concerns about the complexity and benefits of converting to an in-iter ->iomap_next() model, suggesting a more incremental approach by first moving to a single callback, then inline for performance-critical paths, and finally attempting to convert to a pure iterator. He requested a proof-of-concept from Joanne Koong to gauge interest.
review_comments.raw_body:
This does sound like a lot of work, and I'm not a 100% sold on
the benefits, although getting rid of callbacks would be nice.

I'd be tempted to say that as a first step we should move the the
single callback, as a second stop (after converting everyone to
the single callback) to inline for performance critical path, and
then see if we can massage it into a pure iterator without much
downside instead of trying to do everything at once.

As you seem to have the strongest opinion here, I'd love to see
a proof of concept from you to get everyone excited.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the current patch does not address the constness issue and is concerned that the iter private data might not remain constant
review_comments.raw_body:
As in my reply to Gao I missed the constness point.  So I think keeping
the current version should be ok.  I'm a little worried that we might
end up with something not beeing const in the end like the iter private
data, though.

review_comments.author: Gao Xiang
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where iomap_next() returns an error, and requested a check for this condition before calling try_to_unmap()

Reviewer Gao Xiang expressed interest in the patch but noted that it's not a blocker for their current use cases, and suggested exploring its application to other filesystems like btrfs and f2fs.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Christoph,

On 2026/6/26 13:10, Christoph Hellwig wrote:

---

I know it's much better to "show the code" but I also think it's
useful to comment ideas now rather than later in case that anyone
is interested so that it could be useful for their adaption.
I hope I could arrange it in my formal todo list, but I've already
had a bunch of other things (erofs fsdax dedupe support as I told
to you since it impacts workloads, and some overlayfs improvement
and more) in my scheduling list.

Although I tend to play with this idea, it's not a blocker on
my main interested use cases for now.  Also see if some
complicated conversion like btrfs and f2fs can make more related
developers interested in following this way.

Thanks,
Gao Xiang

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig requested that the patch series focus on converting iomap operations from begin/end to an iter model, citing that it is simpler than expected and would allow for a smoother transition before moving on to inlining callbacks.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yeah.  But for now I'd like to get the relatively low hanging fruit
done.  And Joanne showed that moving from begin/end to an iter is
actually way simpler than I thought by having the iomap_process
helper that still does get callbacks, but which get inlined.  So
I'd be really happy to get this done ASAP before we get a lot more
conversions, and also to avoid having a partial transitition where
we need to keep the old ops around for too long.

After that doing the inlining as in patch 3 becomes pretty easy,
so we can do it where it matter like direct I/O.  And also once
we are down to a single callback I think we have a better baseline
for your idea.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author is addressing Horst's concern about performance regressions when using large folios and strictlimiting off, explaining that a commit (494d2f508883) fixed the issue by changing writeback accounting. The author also provides additional benchmark results to support their claim.

Author asked for clarification on reviewer's test environment and results, specifically whether the issue was seen with strictlimiting enabled or disabled, and what test and server were used.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Horst,

Those results are with strictlimiting on. After commit 494d2f508883
('fuse: use default writeback accounting') [1], I didn't see any
performance regressions anymore with large folios + strictlimiting on.
More information on why that commit fixed the issue is in [2].

When I ran the benchmarks last week with strictlimiting off, I saw roughly:
    tmpfs:
      seq,  128k    1174 -> 1648 MiB/s    +40%
      seq,  1M      1261 -> 1845 MiB/s    +46%
      rand, 128k    1148 -> 1638 MiB/s    +43%
      rand, 1M      1273 -> 2065 MiB/s    +62%

    xfs on NVMe:
      seq,  128k     621 ->  740 MiB/s    +19%
      seq,  1M       649 ->  776 MiB/s    +20%
      rand, 128k    1020 -> 1515 MiB/s    +49%
      rand, 1M      1125 -> 1895 MiB/s    +68%

Strict limiting on actually had better performance here, which I think
is because with the small dirty limit, the dirtying and the writeback
happen in parallel instead of more dirty pages accumulating and then
writeback getting kicked off. Because the backing device is so fast,
it didn't cost throughput for the dirtying and the writeback to happen
concurrently. The benchmarks were run with fsync, so everything had to
be flushed before the fio run returned. If the backing device was slow
and writes were bursty and fsync wasn't enforced, I think there'd
probably be better performance with strictlimiting off than on, since
the writer would be throttled to the speed of the backing device with
strictlimtiing on.

---

Are you seeing this on your system with strictlimiting on or off? Is
that with commit 494d2f508883 in your tree? What test and server were
you running? Do you know what the speed of the backing device is?

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/commit/fs/fuse/file.c?id=494d2f508883a6e5c4530e5c6b3c8b2bbfb7318d
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/CAJnrk1ZSaNRr-HWw-hbo2=LmbZiNGZveb0MwxZbPtBDFgg2icQ@mail.gmail.com/

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2 0/2] iomap: add helper to keep uptodate bitmap in sync
Re: [RFC PATCH v1 0/3] iomap: convert to in-iter ->iomap_next() model
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series converts the iomap code to use an in-iter model, where a single callback function is used for both beginning and ending I/O operations. The conversion involves merging two existing functions into one and adding a helper function to process I/O operations. An example of devirtualizing the callbacks on a hot path is also provided to avoid indirect calls through struct iomap_ops. The patch series raises questions about whether this conversion is worth it, given that indirect calls are cheap on modern hardware.

review_comments.author: Gao Xiang
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Gao Xiang noted that the patch introduces a potential deadlock scenario where iomap_next() is called while holding the folio lock, and requested that the lock be dropped before calling try_to_unmap().

reviewer noted that the patch does not fully align with their original in-iter direction and provided a specific example of the design model difference they are concerned about

Reviewer Gao Xiang noted that the recent iomap implementation is still callback-based and requires arranging mapping logic into unique callbacks, which can be inflexible for filesystems needing additional information. He suggested a high-level fs helper library to wrap up page cache management, IO management, and other operations, allowing for more flexibility in handling different types of I/O.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

On 2026/6/26 05:33, Joanne Koong wrote:

---

Thanks for the reply.

I partially agree since it's much closer, but what I meant is
the design model difference as below.

---

If you take a look at the initial commit of iomap ae259a9c8593
("fs: introduce iomap infrastructure"), the starting point was
to provide `struct iomap_ops` (two callbacks) to get the filesystem
extent mapping and unique entrypoints: iomap_file_buffered_write(),
iomap_page_mkwrite() for buffer writes (write and mmap write).

And then iomap applies to DAX, direct I/O, writeback, buffer read,
etc.  basically for block-based filesystems, and that is why
`struct iomap_ops` is always _one important part of iomap core_
until FUSE adapted.


But if we take a look at the overall requirement, I think what we
need is to provide an easy-use high-level fs helper library to
wrap up the following stuffs:

  1. page cache management (mainly sub-page tracking) and DAX pages;

  2. IO management (buffer I/O, direct I/O, DAX access);

  3. others (like fiemap, bmap, lseek, etc.)


For example, most block filesystems could use them of all, and for
FUSE and networking filesystem, basically they only need 1 and
maybe partial 3.

The recent implementation (including ->iomap_next()), is still
a callback-based model, the main loop is inside `fs/iomap` itself,
and you have to:

  - still arrange the mapping logic into an unique callback, which
is .iomap_next() now, if the mapping logic needs more information
rather than `struct inode *`, they have to add private pointer
somewhere.

  - Take a recent example (buffered read refactoring), need
    `struct iomap_read_folio_ctx` for FUSE and maybe other future
    networking filesystems to callback into their filesystem to
    provide new I/O contexts and submit I/Os.

But actually take a simple example of iomap_read_folio, you:
could change into (just a very rough sketch):

  xxxfs_read_folio {
    struct xxxxfs_private priv;

    iomap_iter(&iter) {   /// it could be a macro
        if (iomap_iter_outofrange(&iter))
		xxxfs_map_extent(&iter, &priv);

        // to maintain the page cache <-> struct iomap_folio_state
        // and handle some type of mapping
        if (iomap_read_folio_iter(&iter) == NEED_NEW_IO_CONTEXT) {
            xxxfs_new_io (&iter, &priv);
        }
    }
    if (xxxfs_if_any_available_io(&iter, &priv))
	xxxfs_submit_io(&iter, &priv);
}

In this model, I really think `struct iomap_read_folio_ctx` and
similar doesn't need any more (another set of callback avoidness),
and both xxxfs_map_extent() and xxxfs_new_io()/ xxxfs_submit_io()
can pass in their per-fs private without need to bother
the iomap-core to add various `private` pointers.

and in the iomap_iter() loop you can omit/change/add any part if
it doesn't fulfill to the filesystem timing.

As for "a lot of duplicate code", I think with proper new designs,
the common part could be wrapped up into macros (for example,
for bdev filesystems, xxxfs_new_io() and xxxfs_submit_io() calls
can be wrapped up into macros, and only leave:

	if (iomap_iter_outofrange(&iter))
		xxxfs_map_extent(&iter, &priv);"

can be customized for each fs as the common macro helpers.

It's just my rough thought, as I said in the previous year, I
still don't have a strong tendency but it's my suggestion over
these years.

Anyway since you're actively working/changing on iomap, so
it's fine to just respect your ideas/efforts if you don't
think it's worth, but as a record of my thoughts here again.

Thanks,
Gao Xiang

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig expressed concerns about the complexity and benefits of converting to an in-iter ->iomap_next() model, suggesting a more incremental approach by first moving to a single callback, then inline for performance-critical paths, and finally attempting to convert to a pure iterator. He requested a proof-of-concept from Joanne Koong to gauge interest.
review_comments.raw_body:
This does sound like a lot of work, and I'm not a 100% sold on
the benefits, although getting rid of callbacks would be nice.

I'd be tempted to say that as a first step we should move the the
single callback, as a second stop (after converting everyone to
the single callback) to inline for performance critical path, and
then see if we can massage it into a pure iterator without much
downside instead of trying to do everything at once.

As you seem to have the strongest opinion here, I'd love to see
a proof of concept from you to get everyone excited.

review_comments.author: Gao Xiang
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where iomap_next() returns an error, and requested a check for this condition before calling try_to_unmap()

Reviewer Gao Xiang expressed interest in the patch but noted that it's not a blocker for their current use cases, and suggested exploring its application to other filesystems like btrfs and f2fs.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Christoph,

On 2026/6/26 13:10, Christoph Hellwig wrote:

---

I know it's much better to "show the code" but I also think it's
useful to comment ideas now rather than later in case that anyone
is interested so that it could be useful for their adaption.
I hope I could arrange it in my formal todo list, but I've already
had a bunch of other things (erofs fsdax dedupe support as I told
to you since it impacts workloads, and some overlayfs improvement
and more) in my scheduling list.

Although I tend to play with this idea, it's not a blocker on
my main interested use cases for now.  Also see if some
complicated conversion like btrfs and f2fs can make more related
developers interested in following this way.

Thanks,
Gao Xiang

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Christoph Hellwig requested that the patch series focus on converting iomap operations from begin/end to an iter model, citing that it is simpler than expected and would allow for a smoother transition before moving on to inlining callbacks.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yeah.  But for now I'd like to get the relatively low hanging fruit
done.  And Joanne showed that moving from begin/end to an iter is
actually way simpler than I thought by having the iomap_process
helper that still does get callbacks, but which get inlined.  So
I'd be really happy to get this done ASAP before we get a lot more
conversions, and also to avoid having a partial transitition where
we need to keep the old ops around for too long.

After that doing the inlining as in patch 3 becomes pretty easy,
so we can do it where it matter like direct I/O.  And also once
we are down to a single callback I think we have a better baseline
for your idea.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [RFC PATCH] mm: Avoiding split large folios if swap has no space
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory cgroup charging mechanism to avoid splitting large folios when there is no available swap space. The current implementation tries to charge against the first ancestor that fails, but this can lead to unnecessary splits. Instead, the patch proposes calculating the actual headroom by walking up the hierarchy and taking the minimum of all ancestors' available swap space. This approach aims to relax the strict limit enforcement in favor of a more flexible margin-based calculation.
